does: This paper argues that AI researchers bear responsibility for engaging directly in military AI governance and arms control efforts. It identifies the immediate near-term risk of AI integration into military systems by defense contractors and armament manufacturers as a pressing concern requiring urgent attention. The work contends that while AI safety research typically emphasizes long-term superintelligence risks, this focus inadequately addresses the concrete challenges posed by frontier AI models being deployed in defense applications today. The paper calls for collaborative engagement between AI researchers, military leaders, and arms control diplomacy experts to establish governance frameworks and mitigation strategies for military AI systems.
connects: This work builds on existing arms control literature and nuclear deterrence frameworks, recognizing that historical approaches to managing existential risks through verification protocols and diplomatic treaties offer valuable precedents. It responds to the observation that AI safety research has concentrated heavily on abstract long-term scenarios while leaving near-term military applications underexamined. The paper extends concerns raised in the AI governance and policy literature about industry-defense partnerships, positioning military AI as a domain where established international relations tools can be adapted. It connects the emerging field of AI alignment and safety research to traditional security studies, drawing parallels between nuclear arms control verification challenges and the verification problems posed by AI systems.
enables: This framing enables a bridge between technical AI safety communities and policy institutions, creating pathways for researchers to contribute their expertise to arms control verification and monitoring regimes. It points toward developing technical standards and verification methods for military AI systems that could analogously inform broader AI governance frameworks. The work suggests that combining AI technical knowledge with arms control diplomacy expertise could produce novel verification approaches, transparency mechanisms, and confidence-building measures between nations. Future research could develop concrete technical protocols for auditing military AI systems, establishing international norms around acceptable AI military applications, and creating early warning systems for destabilizing military AI developments.
